title: Stopwatch (millisecond precision, lap times)
shortDescription: High-precision in-browser stopwatch with start/pause, lap recording, and split-time calculation. Millisecond display, monotonic clock for accuracy.
description: Browser-based stopwatch using performance.now() for jitter-free timing. Start, pause, resume, capture laps, and reset. Each lap shows split (delta from previous) plus cumulative time.
metaDescription: Free browser stopwatch with millisecond precision and lap recording. Monotonic timing, no network needed. Useful for sports, cooking, and study sessions.

sections.heading: Why this stopwatch is accurate
paragraphs: Most browser stopwatches use Date.now() and setInterval, which can drift by tens of milliseconds during a session. This implementation uses performance.now() — a monotonic clock that doesn't jump backward when the system clock is adjusted. Combined with requestAnimationFrame for display updates, accuracy stays within ±1 ms.

If you switch tabs, the displayed time may freeze briefly because browsers throttle background work. The internal clock keeps running correctly — when you switch back, the display catches up to the actual elapsed time.

sections.heading: Lap times and split times
paragraphs: When you press Lap, the current cumulative time is recorded. The displayed split is the delta from the previous lap: in a 5×400m run, each split is one 400m segment time, while the cumulative is total elapsed since start.
Most lap-list users care about splits, not cumulative. We show both. Cumulative also makes it easy to identify the best individual segment — useful for athletes tracking interval consistency.

sections.heading: Common uses
paragraphs: Sports: track lap times for running, swimming, cycling intervals. Smaller is better; consistency between splits is a sign of good pacing.
Cooking: time multiple steps simultaneously by using laps as 'check points' (mix added, dough resting started, etc.).
Study/work: timeboxing focused work sessions. Pair with our Pomodoro timer for structured breaks.
Process timing: measure duration of any task — software builds, manual data entry, customer support resolution. Use the export-friendly lap list for later review.

howTo.name: Capture laps
howTo.text: Click Lap during the run. Each lap is recorded with split and cumulative time.

howTo.name: Pause / Resume
howTo.text: Click Pause to hold; click Resume to continue from the same time.

howTo.name: Reset
howTo.text: Clears all laps and returns to 0.

faq.q: Will the timer keep running if I close the tab?
faq.a: No — closing the tab clears the in-memory state. To preserve a long timing session, take a screenshot of the running display or copy the lap list before closing.

faq.q: How accurate is millisecond precision in practice?
faq.a: performance.now() resolution is browser-dependent: most browsers limit to 1ms or 0.1ms for security reasons (timing attack mitigation). This is plenty for human-scale timing — far more accurate than human reaction time (~250ms).

faq.q: Why does the display update at 60fps but show milliseconds?
faq.a: The internal clock is precise; the display is rendered ~60 times per second so you see a smoothly updating millisecond field. Each frame computes the latest elapsed value from performance.now().

faq.q: Can I save a lap session?
faq.a: Not directly — copy the lap list manually. Saving sessions to local storage is on the roadmap.

faq.q: What's the difference between this and a phone stopwatch?
faq.a: Browser-based, no app install needed. Equally accurate for human-scale timing. Phone stopwatch survives screen lock; browser tab needs to stay open (but doesn't need focus).
No. Everything runs locally; no laps or times are transmitted.

faq.q: Why does my lap show 0 milliseconds?
faq.a: If you tap Lap immediately after Start, the elapsed time may round to 0 with display precision. Run for at least a fraction of a second between laps.
Yes, down to ~1ms. For nanosecond timing (chip benchmarking) you'd need different tooling, but for any human-observable event this is precise enough.
